               The Secretary of Labor has determined that
               the publication of this periodical is necessary
               in the transaction of the public business re-
               quired by law of this Department. Use of funds
               for printing this periodical has been approved
               by the Director of the Office of Management
               and Budget through February 28, 1981. Con-
               trolled circulation postage paid at Riverdale,
               Md.

                      For coverage of series, see footnote 1, table B-2.                                                                  p=preliminary (applicable to earnings data only).

                      Spendable earnings are calculated by taking the average weekly pay for all production
               or nonsupervisory jobs, both full-time and part-time, and then deducting social security and
               Federal income taxes applicable to a single worker or to a married worker with three depen-
               dents who earned this amount (see Explanatory Notes for the establishment data in the back of
               this publication). A technical note on the calculation and uses of the spendable earnings series
               is available on request.

               Reliability of the eetlmatee                                                     Sampling error. The standard error is primarily a measure of sampl-
                                                                                            ing variability, that is, of the variation that occurs by chance because a
                  There are two types of errors possible in an estimate based on a          sample rather than the entire population is surveyed. The sample
               sample survey—sampling and nonsampling. The standard errors pro-             estimate and its estimated standard error enables one to construct con-
               vided primarily indicate the magnitude of the sampling error. They            fidence intervals, ranges that would include the average of all possible
               also partially measure the effect of some nonsampling errors in              samples with a known probability. For example, if all possible
               response and enumeration but do not measure any systematic biases in         samples were selected, each of these surveyed under essentially the
               the data.                                                                     same general conditions and using the same sample design, and an
                  Nonsampling errors. The full extent of nonsampling error is               estimate and its estimated error were calculated from each sample,
               unknown, but special studies have been conducted to qualify some             then:

               fect of nonsampling error should be small on estimates of relative           ror below the estimate to one standard error above the estimate would
               change, such as month-to-month change. Estimates of monthly levels           include the average result of all possible samples.
               would be more severely affected by the nonsampling error.                        2. Approximately 90 percent of the intervals froml.6 standard er-
                  Nonsampling errors in surveys can be attributed to many sources,          rors below the estmate to 1.6 standard errors above the estimate would
               e.g., inability to obtain information about all cases in the sample,          include the average of all possible samples.
               definitional difficulties, differences in the interpretation of questions,       3. Approximately 95 percent of the intervals from 2 standard errors
               inability or unwillingness of respondents to provide correct informa-        below the estimate to 2 standard errors above the estimate would in-
               tion, inability to recall information, errors made in collection such as     clude the average result of all possible samples.

               State agencies. State agencies mail the forms to the establishments and      Security Agencies are also excluded.
               examine the returns for consistency, accuracy, and completeness. The            Persons on establishment payrolls who are on paid sick leave (when

                                                                                           Industry hours snd earnings
               Shuttle schedules
                                                                                               Average hours and earnings data are derived from reports of
                  Two types of data collection schedules are used: Form BLS                payrolls and hours for production and related workers in manufactur-
               790—Report on Employment, Payroll, and Hours; and Form DL                   ing and mining, construction workers in construction, and nonsuper-
               1219—Monthly Report on Labor Turnover. The collection agency                visory employees in private service-producing industries. An exception
               returns the schedule to the respondent each month so that the next          to the definitions below are the statistics on hours and earnings of
               month's data can be entered on the space allotted for that month. This      Federal Government employees, reported in table C-3, which are for
                "shuttle" procedure increases comparability and accuracy of                 all Federal employees, both supervisory and nonsupervisory, for the
               reporting, since the respondent can see the figures that have been           entire calendar month. When the pay period reported is longer than 1
               reported for previous months.                                                week, figures are reduced to a weekly basis.
                  Form BLS 790 provides for entry of data on the total number of              Production and related workers include working supervisors and all
               'full- and part-time workers on the payrolls of nonagricultural             nonsupervisory workers (including group leaders and trainees) engag-
               establishments and, for most industries, employment, payroll, and           ed in fabricating, processing, assembling, inspection, receiving,
               hours of production and related workers or nonsupervisory workers           storage, handling, packing, warehousing, shipping, maintenance,
                for the pay period which includes the 12th of the month. Form DL           repair, janitorial and guard services, product development, auxiliary
                1219 provides for the collection of information on the total number of     production for plant's own use (e.g., power plant), and recordkeeping
